**Cape Canaveral** is a city in [Brevard County, Florida](/wiki/Brevard_County,_Florida "Brevard County, Florida"). It is part of the [Palm Bay](/wiki/Palm_Bay,_Florida "Palm Bay, Florida")–[Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne,_Florida "Melbourne, Florida")–[Titusville](/wiki/Titusville,_Florida "Titusville, Florida") [Metropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/Palm_Bay-Melbourne-Titusville,_Florida_Metropolitan_Statistical_Area "Palm Bay-Melbourne-Titusville, Florida Metropolitan Statistical Area"). The population was 9,912 at the 2020 US census.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Brevard County § History](/wiki/Brevard_County#History "Brevard County")

After the establishment of a lighthouse in 1848,[[5]](#cite_note-5) a few families moved into the area and a small but stable settlement was born. As the threat of [Seminole](/wiki/Seminole "Seminole") Indian attacks became increasingly unlikely, other settlers began to move into the area around the Indian River. Post offices and small community stores with postal facilities were established at Canaveral, Canaveral Harbor and Artesia. It is thought the Artesia post office was so named for the ground water of artesian springs that are prevalent in the area.

In 1890, a group of alumni from Harvard University students established a hunters gun club called the Canaveral Harvard Club with a holding of over 18,000 acres (7,300 ha). Their game hunts helped clear the wilderness for other settlers to move into the area.[[6]](#cite_note-Text-6)

In the early 1920s, a group of [Orlando](/wiki/Orlando,_Florida "Orlando, Florida") journalists invested more than $150,000 in the beach acreage that now encompasses the area of presidentially named streets in Cape Canaveral. They called their development Journalista (now Avon-by-the-Sea) in honor of their trade.[[6]](#cite_note-Text-6) A wooden bridge linking [Merritt Island](/wiki/Merritt_Island,_Florida "Merritt Island, Florida") with the area had just been constructed. The developers anticipated a growing number of seasonal visitors.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At that time, fishermen, retirees, and descendants of Captain [Mills Burnham](/wiki/Mills_O._Burnham "Mills O. Burnham") —the original official keeper of the [Cape Canaveral Light](/wiki/Cape_Canaveral_Light "Cape Canaveral Light")—resided in the northern part of the present city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Due to the hardships caused by the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), many investors defaulted on their holdings. Much of this land was recovered by newspaper owner R.B. Brossier and his son, Dickson, after they sold their Orlando home and used the remaining $4,500 to purchase much of the Avon area.

In the 1930s, archaeologists from Yale University surveyed various Native American sites in the area.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1951, anthropologist Irvine Rouse of Yale University visited the area and performed research.[[7]](#cite_note-RiverArchaeology-7)

By 1958, the workforce and the economy had grown with the space exploration program. At that time, state statute allowed an adjacent city to annex an unincorporated area without a vote of the residents. Local property owners were concerned that [Cocoa Beach](/wiki/Cocoa_Beach,_Florida "Cocoa Beach, Florida") might annex them. Landowners felt that Cocoa Beach had more city debt and higher land taxes than they wished to support.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The City of Cape Canaveral started in 1961, when a committee was formed to [incorporate](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation").[[6]](#cite_note-Text-6) Due to paperwork delays the city charter was made into bill 167 and approved by the Florida State Legislature in Tallahassee on May 16, 1963.[[9]](#cite_note-TheHistoryCC-9)

In 1967, the annual Sun and Space Festival was started. It had flyovers and a parade that included a stop at the newly opened Museum of Sunken Treasure.[[6]](#cite_note-Text-6) This contained artifacts from the [1715 Treasure Fleet](/wiki/1715_Treasure_Fleet "1715 Treasure Fleet").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

An annual celebration was started on October 9, 1990, The Patriot's Day Parade in honor of the last naval battle of the American Revolution that was fought off the Cape Canaveral coast in 1783.[[6]](#cite_note-Text-6)

In 2000, *The Washington Post* reported that the city's divorce rate was the highest in the country, 22%. It was the same rate in 2018.[[11]](#cite_note-ft180528-11)

At a Heritage Day event in March 2013 part of the festivities for the city's 50th anniversary included author [Jay Barbree](/wiki/Jay_Barbree "Jay Barbree") who delivered an oral history of the early days. On the 50th anniversary date of May 16, 2013, a 50-year time capsule was sealed and a pictorial postmark of the city's anniversary was stamped.

In 2017, the city won "Most Fit City" in the Mayor's Fitness Challenge, hosted by [Health First](/wiki/Health_First "Health First").[[12]](#cite_note-Fitness-12)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

The city of Cape Canaveral is located on a [barrier island](/wiki/Barrier_island "Barrier island") on the Atlantic coast of Florida. It is due south of the geographical feature [Cape Canaveral](/wiki/Cape_Canaveral "Cape Canaveral"). It is separated from the mainland by the [Banana River](/wiki/Banana_River "Banana River"), [Merritt Island](/wiki/Merritt_Island,_Florida "Merritt Island, Florida") and the [Indian River](/wiki/Indian_River_(Florida) "Indian River (Florida)") from east to west.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 2.3 square miles (6.0 km2). 2.3 square miles (6.0 km2) of it is land and 0.04 square miles (0.10 km2) of it (0.85%) is water.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Climate")]

Cape Canaveral has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa* in the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")). It has hot wet summers, and warm winters with moderate rainfall. In late summer and fall tropical cyclones can brush the area. On August 20, 2008, [Tropical Storm Fay](/wiki/Tropical_Storm_Fay_(2008)#Florida "Tropical Storm Fay (2008)") dropped 20.03 inches (50.9 cm) of rain.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[3]](#cite_note-GR2-3) of 2000, there were 8,829 people, 5,066 households, and 2,097 families residing in the city. The population density was 3,788.0 inhabitants per square mile (1,462.6/km2). There were 6,641 housing units at an average density of 2,849.3 units per square mile (1,100.1 units/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 94.68% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 1.43% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.32% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 1.70% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.06% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 0.42% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 1.40% from two or more races. Out of all of which [Hispanics](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latinos](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of constituted 3.48% of the population, regardless of race.

In 2000, there were 5,066 households, out of which 11.9% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 30.7% were married couples living together, 7.4% had a female householder with no husband present, and 58.6% were non-families. 47.1% of all households were made up of individuals, and 13.8% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 1.74 and the average family size was 2.41.

In 2000, in the city, the population was spread out, with 11.3% under the age of 18, 6.4% from 18 to 24, 30.4% from 25 to 44, 28.8% from 45 to 64, and 23.1% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 46 years. For every 100 females, there were 109.1 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 107.7 males.

In 2000, the median income for a household in the city was $30,858, and the median income for a family was $43,109. Males had a median income of $33,571 versus $22,423 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $23,537. About 9.2% of families and 11.6% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 28.7% of those under age 18 and 7.1% of those age 65 or over.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economy")]

Cape Canaveral has a cross section of both single family and multifamily residences. A number of hotels and time shares are located in the area. Many residents work in the [service industry](/wiki/Service_industry "Service industry"), engineering firms, and at the [Kennedy Space Center](/wiki/Kennedy_Space_Center "Kennedy Space Center") to the north.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Workforce

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Workforce")]

In 2007, the average size of Cape Canaveral's labor force was 5,824. Of that group, 5,533 were employed and 291 were unemployed, for an unemployment rate of 5%.[[22]](#cite_note-edc-22)

### Housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Housing")]

In 2008, no [building permits](/wiki/Building_permits "Building permits") were issued. This was down from five permits for six units in 2007, which was down from 19 permits for 42 units in 2006.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The [median home price](/wiki/Median_home_price "Median home price") in 2007 was $215,000.[[22]](#cite_note-edc-22)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[Tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism") plays a major role in the economy as in any Florida beachside community. The largest hotel in Brevard County is located in the city. It has 284 rooms and 30,000 square feet (2,800 m2) of meeting space.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Government")]

Cape Canaveral is run by a [council–manager government](/wiki/Council%E2%80%93manager_government "Council–manager government"). The [City Council](/wiki/City_Council "City Council") consists of five members, including the mayor. All seats on the City Council are filled by [nonpartisan](/wiki/Non-partisan_democracy "Non-partisan democracy") election as outlined in the City Charter.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The City Manager is an appointed position that acts as the city's administrative leader and carries out the council's acts and directives.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

* Mayor – Wes Morrison[[27]](#cite_note-Council-27)
* Mayor Pro Tem – Mickie Kellum
* Council Member – Kim Davis
* Council Member – Kay Jackson
* Council Member – Don Willis
* City Manager (appointed) – Keith Touchberry[[28]](#cite_note-Man-28)
* City Clerk (appointed) – Esther Coulson[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In 2007, the city had a taxable real estate base of $1.46 billion.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In 2009–2010, the city paid $833,100 for [solid waste disposal](/wiki/Municipal_solid_waste "Municipal solid waste"). This was furnished at a cost from $4.95 to $7.38 monthly per residence.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Federally, Cape Canaveral is part of [Florida's 8th congressional district](/wiki/Florida%27s_8th_congressional_district "Florida's 8th congressional district"), represented by Republican [Bill Posey](/wiki/Bill_Posey "Bill Posey"), elected in 2008.

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Transportation")]

The primary transportation is by road.

* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Florida_A1A.svg/20px-Florida_A1A.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Florida_A1A.svg) [SR A1A](/wiki/Florida_State_Road_A1A "Florida State Road A1A"), known officially as Astronaut Boulevard in the central and northern parts of the town and Atlantic Avenue in the southern parts, is the main road, running north–south within the city. Major intersections include George King Boulevard ([diamond interchange](/wiki/Diamond_interchange "Diamond interchange")), Central Boulevard, and Atlantic Avenue.
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Florida_528.svg/20px-Florida_528.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Florida_528.svg) [SR 528](/wiki/Florida_State_Road_528 "Florida State Road 528") is a [controlled-access highway](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") that connects the city with the mainland. It is concurrent with SR A1A until George King Boulevard, where SR 528 then ends, and SR A1A becomes a normal surface road.

Public transportation is provided by [Space Coast Area Transit](/wiki/Space_Coast_Area_Transit "Space Coast Area Transit") (SCAT). The #9 Beach Trolley bus line circles through Cape Canaveral and runs down to Cocoa Beach and connects with other SCAT bus lines serving Brevard County.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Water

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Canaveral,_Florida&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Water")]

Residents obtain potable water from the city of Cocoa. A single potable water line from Cocoa runs under the Sykes Creek Bridge at Sea Ray Drive.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)
